name: Turia Gardens and Valencia Historic Center Loop
name_original: Los Jardines del Turia — Circular por el casco histórico de Valencia
description: Explore Valencia's historic center on this easy 4.0-mile (6.5 km) hike through the Turia Garden, featuring historic sites and lush greenery.
region_intro: Hiking around Valence offers access to diverse landscapes, situated between the hills of Ardèche and the Vercors mountains. The region features a mix of forests, rolling hills, and river valleys, providing varied terrain for outdoor activities. Natural features include the Monts du Matin with its cliffs and panoramas, as well as gorges and rural countryside. The Drôme region, where Valence is located, is characterized by green hills and peaceful rivers. Best hiking trails around Valence The most popular hiking route is Turia Gardens and Valencia Historic Center Loop, a 4.0 miles (6.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 40 minutes to complete. This easy route explores urban green spaces and historic areas. Another top favourite among local hikers is The Paseo Maritimo of Valencia, an easy 4.8 miles (7.7 km) path. This trail follows the coastline, offering views of the sea. Local hikers also love the Gola de Pujol and Pujol Lake Loop, a 3.1 miles (5.0 km) trail leading through natural parkland, often completed in about 1 hour 16 minutes. Hiking around Valence is defined by diverse environments including forests, hills, and river paths. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy strolls to more challenging treks. The routes in Valence are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 800 reviews. More than 10,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Valence's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Valence?
A: There are over 390 hiking trails around Valence, offering a wide variety of experiences. This includes 246 easy routes, 138 moderate trails, and 11 more challenging options, ensuring there's something for every skill level.

Q: Are there easy hikes with scenic views near Valence?
A: Yes, many easy hikes around Valence offer beautiful views. The region is known for its rolling hills and proximity to the Vercors mountains, providing panoramic vistas. For example, the Monts du Matin, at the foot of the Vercors, feature paths with breathtaking panoramas. Even within Valence, parks like Jouvet Park offer views of the Vercors.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking near Valence?
A: Hiking around Valence offers incredibly diverse landscapes. You can explore the foothills of the majestic Vercors Mountains, discover forests and tranquil hills, or follow paths along peaceful rivers in the Drôme region. Natural features like the Gorges de la Combe d'Oyans and rural countryside with views of the Vercors plateau are also common.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Valence?
A: Yes, circular routes are popular in the Valence area. Many trails are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example of a loop trail is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e828428605/gola-de-pujol-and-pujol-lake-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gola de Pujol and Pujol Lake Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through natural parkland.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Valence?
A: The hiking trails around Valence are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 870 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse environments, from urban green spaces to mountain foothills, and the variety of options suitable for all abilities.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options near Valence?
A: Valence offers many family-friendly hiking options. The region's diverse environments, including forests, hills, and urban parks, provide opportunities for all ages. Easy, shorter routes are plentiful, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e827099215/turia-gardens-and-valencia-historic-center-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Turia Gardens and Valencia Historic Center Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which explores urban green spaces and historic areas, perfect for a leisurely family outing.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails around Valence?
A: Many trails around Valence are suitable for dogs, though it's always recommended to check specific trail regulations or local signage. Generally, the natural parks and rural paths are welcoming to well-behaved dogs on a leash, allowing you and your pet to enjoy the diverse landscapes together.

Q: What is the typical duration of hikes around Valence?
A: Hikes around Valence vary greatly in duration. Many easy routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e827267629/the-paseo-maritimo-of-valencia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Paseo Maritimo of Valencia&lt;/a&gt;, can be completed in under 2 hours. Longer, more challenging treks in the Vercors foothills might take several hours. With over 390 routes, you can find options ranging from short strolls to full-day adventures.

Q: Are there any trails that follow rivers or coastlines near Valence?
A: Yes, the region offers trails that follow waterways. The Drôme region is characterized by peaceful rivers, providing scenic paths. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e827267629/the-paseo-maritimo-of-valencia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Paseo Maritimo of Valencia&lt;/a&gt; follows the coastline, offering beautiful views of the sea, while the extensive Viarhôna cycle route also accommodates pedestrians along the Rhône.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks to look out for on hikes?
A: When hiking near Valence, you can look forward to several notable natural features. The Vercors Mountains provide magnificent backdrops and challenging trails. The Monts du Matin offer striking cliffs and rolling hills. You might also encounter natural gorges like the Gorges de la Combe d'Oyans, and various forests and lakes throughout the Valence Romans area.

Q: Is public transport accessible to hiking trailheads near Valence?
A: Valence has a public transport network that can provide access to some trailheads, especially those closer to urban or suburban areas. For trails further afield, particularly in the Vercors foothills or more remote natural areas, personal transport might be more convenient. It's advisable to check local bus routes for specific trail access points.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers around Valence?
A: Yes, for experienced trekkers, Valence offers challenging hikes, particularly in the nearby Vercors Mountains. The GR 93 long-distance hiking path, for instance, originates in the Rhône Valley and traverses the Vercors, leading to demanding terrain and significant elevation changes. There are 11 routes classified as 'difficult' in the area, providing a good test for seasoned hikers.
tips: Great atmosphere. The offer is very large.

An estimated 27,000 colored flowers adorn this bridge, which are replaced 4 times a year so that the flowers are always in bloom. That makes the flower bridge perhaps the most photogenic bridge of them all. If you cycle over this, you will drive through a veritable sea of flowers. Or take a seat on one of the many benches on the side of the bridge among the flowers.

This immense garden was created in the old bed of the Turia river, which was diverted to avoid the constant flooding that the city suffered. After the great flood of October 14, 1957 that destroyed the city, the bed of the Turia was diverted to the south of Valencia, leaving a significant strip of land that crosses the city from east to west and surrounds the historic center. When the gardens were opened in 1986, several urban planners and landscape architects designed the different stretches of the riverbed, imitating the former landscape of the river and creating a unique course planted and equipped with palm and orange trees, fountains and pine trees, aromatic plants and ponds, sports fields and rose gardens.

The Serranos towers are, together with the Quart portal, the only old entrances to the wall of Valencia that remain standing. The opulence of its architecture shows more of a symbolic character than a defensive one. Built in the 14th century, they were intended to show everyone who entered the city how important Valencia was in the Middle Ages.

The Fuente del Agua y de la Acequia de Valencia is located in the Plaza de la Virgen, in front of the Puerta de los Apóstoles and the Basilica de los Desamparados. It is a beautiful fountain that pleasantly decorates this pretty pedestrian square in the old quarter of the city.

The remodeling works, which began in April 2022, have turned the square into a diaphanous and accessible space, enhancing the city's historical heritage, and offering a renewed perspective of the Micalet, the baroque door of the Cathedral and Santa Caterina.

You can enter the Plaza Redonda along four streets that converge in a welcoming space, full of light and from which, if you stand in the center near the fountain, you will see a new and beautiful perspective of the Baroque-Rococo bell tower style of Saint Catalina, which extends over the three floors of the round building. One of the city's main tourist attractions.
It is one of the Gothic churches in the city of Valencia built in the cathedral quarter on an old mosque.

The Silk Exchange is one of the city's typical old buildings, and also one of the most famous monuments of Civic Gothic that Europe has to offer. On June 4, 1931, the building was declared a National Historical-Artistic Monument and in 1996 it was declared a World Heritage Site by UNESCO.

The largest fresh produce market in Europe not only has a delicious and varied food offering, but is also housed in one of the city's most iconic and modernist buildings. Covering an area of more than 8,000 square meters, it is full of decorative references to the products irrigated horticultural regions and gardens of Valencia. The structure consists of tiles, large windows and iron columns reminiscent of the Eiffel Tower, making it an architectural masterpiece not to be missed.

The Plaza del Ayuntamiento is the largest and most famous square in the center of Valencia. The triangular square has a beautiful fountain and is surrounded by buildings from the 19th and early 20th centuries. It gives its name to the largest building on this square, namely the Valencia Town Hall (Ayuntamiento). During your city trip to Valencia you are guaranteed to encounter this lively car-free square.

faqs.question: How do I get to the starting point of the route?
faqs.answer: The Turia Gardens are easily accessible throughout Valencia. For this specific circular route, you can start at various points along the park, which runs through the city center. Public transport options like buses and metro are plentiful, with many stops adjacent to the gardens. If driving, there are several underground parking garages in the city center near the park, though specific parking for the gardens themselves is limited to street parking which can be challenging to find.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular route?
faqs.answer: The terrain throughout the Turia Gardens is predominantly flat with smoothly paved paths, making it very easy to navigate. It features dedicated lanes for walking, jogging, and cycling, ensuring a comfortable experience for all activities. This route is ideal for leisurely strolls rather than rugged hiking.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and landmarks can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: This route offers a diverse blend of natural beauty and architectural marvels. You'll walk among palm trees, orange trees, and rose gardens, enjoying the refreshing atmosphere of Spain's largest urban green space. Key landmarks you'll encounter include the historic Bridge of Flowers, the impressive Serranos Towers, the beautiful Plaza de la Virgen, and the iconic Valencia Cathedral. You'll also pass by the Queen's Square and the unique Plaza Redonda.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families and beginners?
faqs.answer: Absolutely! With its easy difficulty, flat and paved paths, and minimal elevation gain, this route is perfect for all ages and fitness levels, including families with children. The Turia Gardens themselves are very family-friendly, featuring playgrounds and open spaces for recreation.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit the Turia Gardens?
faqs.answer: The Turia Gardens are enjoyable year-round. Spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) offer the most pleasant temperatures for walking. Summer can be hot, but the park's trees provide shade, and early mornings or late afternoons are ideal. Winter is generally mild and still suitable for a stroll.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed in the Turia Gardens, but they must be kept on a leash at all times. Please be mindful of other park users and clean up after your pet to help keep the gardens beautiful for everyone.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to access the Turia Gardens?
faqs.answer: No, the Turia Gardens are a public urban park, and there are no entrance fees or permits required to access them. It's freely open to everyone to enjoy.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route through the Turia Gardens and historic center intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the El Camí IT5TR6 El Camí del Sud: de Dénia a Castelló de la Plana (44%), El Camí IT5TR7 El Camí del Sud: de Dénia a Castelló de la Plana (42%), and the Circuit 5K Jardí del Túria (40%). It also briefly touches parts of the Camino de Santiago de Levante and La Conquista de Valencia.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through the historic center of Valencia and along the Turia Gardens, you'll find numerous cafes, restaurants, and shops. A notable spot is Down Monkey Business Mercado de Colón, located near the route, offering a great place for a break.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this walk?
faqs.answer: Given the easy nature of the route, comfortable walking shoes are essential. Depending on the weather, bring water, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and perhaps a light jacket. A camera is also recommended to capture the beautiful scenery and landmarks.

faqs.question: Is the route accessible for strollers or wheelchairs?
faqs.answer: Yes, due to its flat, smoothly paved paths and wide lanes, the route through the Turia Gardens is highly accessible for strollers and wheelchairs, making it an inclusive option for everyone.
